title: 第 25 節
en: 11. " O Fathers, you who have been consumed by Agni, come here, sit down on your seats, you kind guides ! Eat of the offerings which we have placed on the turf, and then grant us wealth and strong offspring !

12. "O Agni, O 6^atavadas,* at our request thou hast carried the offerings, having first rendered them sweet. Thou gavest them to the Fathers, and they fed on their share. Eat also, O God, the proffered oblations "

13. "The Fathers who are here, and the Fathers who are not here, those whom we know, and those whom we know not, thou, 6^atavedas, knowest how many they are, accept the well-made sacrifice with the sacrifical portions !

14. " To those who, whether burnt by fire or not burnt by fire, rejoice in their share in the midst of heaven, grant thou, O King, that their body may take that life which they wish for ! " t Distinct from the worship offered to these primitive ancestors, is the reverence which from an early time was felt to be due by children to their departed father, soon also to their grandfather, and great grandfather. The ceremonies in which these more personal feelings found expression were of a more domestic character, and allowed therefore of greater local variety.

It would be quite impossible to give here even an V£:dA and VEDA N'T a. 2^g abstract only of the minute regulations which have been preserved to us in the Brahma«as, the »Srauta, G//hya, and SamayaMrika Sutras, the Law*books, and a mass of latter manuals on the performance of endless rites, all intended to honor the Departed. Such are the minute prescriptions as to times and seasons, as to altars and offerings, as to the number and shape of the sacrificial vessels, as to the proper postures of the sacrificers, and the different arrangements of the vessels, that it is extremely difficult to catch hold of what we really care for, namely, the thoughts and intentions of those who first devised all these intricacies. Much has been written on this class of sacrifices by European scholars also, beginning with Colebrooke's excellent essays on " The Religious Ceremonies of the Hindus," first published in the Asiatic Researches, vol. v, Calcutta, 1798. But when we ask the simple question, What was the thought from whence all this outward ceremonial sprang, and what was the natural craving of the human heart which it seemed to satisfy, we hardly get an intelligible answer anywhere. It is true that ^raddhas continue to be performed all over India to the present day, but we know how widely the modern ceremonial has diverged from the rules laid down in the old 6'astras, and it is quite clear from the descriptions given to us by recent travellers that no one can understand the purport even of these survivals of the old ceremonial, unless he understands Sanskrit and can read the old Sutras. We are indeed told in full detail how the cakes were made which the Spirits were supposed to eat, how many stalks of grass were to be used on which they had to be offered, how long each stalk ^40 WirA T CAN INDIA TEA Ctt t/S f ought to be, and in what direction it should be held. V All the things which teach us nothing are explained to us in abundance, but the few things which the true scholar really cares for are passed over, as if they had no interest to us at all, and have to be discovered under heaps of rubbish.
In order to gain a little light, I think we ought to distinguish between —
1. The daily ancestral' sacrifice, the Pitr/ya^la, as one of the five Great Sacrifices (Mahaya^/^as) ;
2. The monthly ancestral sacrifice, the Pi;/</a-pitr/ya^;1a, as part of the New and Full-moon sacrifice ;
3. The funeral ceremonies on the death of a householder ;

4. The Agapes, or feasts of love and charity, commonly called 5raddhas, at which food and other charitable gifts were bestowed on deserving persons in memory of the deceased ancestors. The name of »Sraddha belongs properly to this last class only, but it has been transferred to the second and third class of sacrifices also, because 5raddha formed an important part in them.

The daily Pitr/ya^a or Ancestorworship is one of the five sacrifices, sometimes called the Great Sacrifices,* which every married man ought to perform day by day. They are mentioned in the Gnhyaslatras (A^v. III. i), as Devaya^;1a, for the Devas, Bhutaya^la, for animals etc., Pitnya^;1a, for the Fathers, Brahmaya^-^au, for Brahman, i. e. study of the Veda, and Manushyaya^a, for men, i. e. hospitality, etc.
ana Grihya-sfitras III. i, i ; Paraskara GrzTiya-sutras II. 9, i; Apastamba. Dharma-sAtras, translated by Biihler, pp. 47 seq.
VEDA AND VEDANTA. 241 Manu (III. 70) tells us the same, namely, that a married man has five great religious duties to perform :
1. The Brahma-sacrifice, i. e. the studying and teaching of the Veda (sometimes called Ahuta).
2. The Pitn-sacrifice, i. e. the offering of cakes and water to the Manes (sometimes called Prai-ita).
3. The Deva-sacrifice, i. e. the offering of oblations to the Gods (sometimes called Huta).
4. The Bhuta-sacrifice, i. e. the giving of food to living creatures (sometimes called Prahuta).

5. The Manushya-sacrifice. i. e. the receiving of guests with hospitality (sometimes called Brahmyahuta.)* The performance of this daily Pitnya^a seems to have been extremely simple. The householder had to put his sacred cord on the right shoulder, to say " Svadha to the Fathers," and to throw the remains of certain offerings towards the South.!

The human impulse to this sacrifice, if sacrifice it can be called, is clear enough. The five " great sacrifices " comprehended in early times the v/hole duty of man from day to day. They were connected with his daily meal. J When this meal was preparing, and before he could touch it himself, he was to offer something to the Gods, a Vaij-vadeva offering , in which the chief deities were Agni fire. Soma the VivS-ve Devas, Dhanvantari, a kind of Aesculapius, Kuhii and Anumati (phases of the moon), Pra^pati, lord of called Huta, ahuta, prahuta, prMiSt t Ajv. G-rihya-siitras I. 3. 10. | Manu III. 117-11S, Manu III. 85.
2 42 ^^J'A T CAN INDIA TEA CII USf creatures, Dydvd-pnthivt, Heaven and Earth, and SvislVaknt, the fire on the hearth.

After having thus satisfied the Gods in the four quarters, the householder had to throw some oblations into the open air, which were intended for animals, and in some cases for invisible beings, ghosts and such like. Then he was to remember the Departed, the Pit^/s, with some offerings ; but even after having done this he was not yet to begin his own repast, unless he had also given something to strangers (atithis).

When all this had been fulfilled, and when, besides, the householder, as we should say, had said his daily prayers, or repeated what he had learnt of the Veda, then and then only was he in harmony with the world that surrounded him, the five Great Sacrifices had been performed by him, and he was free from all the sins arising from a thoughtless and selfish life.

This Pitnya^^a, as one of the five daily sacrifices, is described in the Brahma/^as, the Gn"hya and Samaya/^arika Sutras, and of course, in the legal Sa;;2hit^s. Rajendralal Mitra * informs us that " orthodox Brahmans to this day profess to observe all these five ceremonies, but that in reality only the offerings to the gods and manes are strictly observed, while the reading is completed by the repetition of the Gayatri only, and charity and feeding of animals are casual and uncertain."

Quite different from this simple daily ancestral offering is the Pitr^ya^^a or Pi;2<^a-pitnya^^a, which forms part of many of the statutable sacrifices, and, first of all, of the New and Full-Moon sacrifice. Here again the human motive is intelligible enough.

VEDA AND VEDANTA. 243 It was the contemplation of the regular course of nature, the discovery of order in the coming and going of the heavenly bodies, the growing confidence in some ruling power of the world which lifted man's thoughts from his daily work to higher regions, and filled his heart with a desire to approach these higher powers with praise, thanksgiving, and offerings. And it was at such moments as the waning of the moon that his thoughts would most naturally turn to those whose life had waned, whose bright faces were no longer visible on earth, his fathers or ancestors. Therefore at the very beginning of the New-Moon sacrifice, we are told in the Brahma;^as f and in the 5rauta-sutras, that a Pitr2ya^;1a, a sacrifice to the Fathers, has to be performed. A iTaru or pie had to be prepared in the Dakshi^^agni, the southern fire, and the offerings, consisting of water and round cakes (pi^^^as), were especially dedicated to father, grandfather and great-grandfather, while the wife of the sacificer, if she wished for a son, was allowed to eat one of the cakes. t Simil?;/)' ^.cestral offerings took place during other sacrificeb ^y, of which the New and Full-Moon sacrifices form the general type.

It may be quite true that these two kinds of ancestral sacrifices have the same object and share the same name, but their character is different; and if, as has often been the case, they are mixed up together, we lose the most important lessons which t See Y\nd2c^\tri'jz.ghz.t von Dr. O. Donner, 1S70. The restriction to three ancestors, father, grandfather, and gr^-grandfather, occurs in the Va^asaneyi-saz^hita,. XIX. 36 — 37.

244 WHAT CAN INDIA TEACH us f a study of the ancient ceremonial should teach us. I cannot describe the difference between those two Pitr/ya^^as more decisively than by pointing out that the former was performed by the father of a family, or, if we may say so, by a layman, the latter by a regular priest, or a class of priests, selected by the sacrificer to act in his behalf. As the Hindus themselves would put it, the former is a gr^'hya, a domestic, the later a j^rauta, a priestly ceremony. * We now come to a third class of ceremonies which are likewise domestic and personal, but which differ from the two preceding ceremonies by their occasional character, I mean the funeral, as distinct from the ancestral ceremonies. In one respect these funeral ceremonies may represent an earlier phase of worship than the daily and monthly ancestral sacrifices. They lead up to them, and, as it were, prepare the departed for their future dignity as Pitn's or Ancestors. On the other hand, the conception of Ancestors in general must have existed before any departed person could have been raised to that rank and I therefore preferred to describe the ancestral sacrifices first.

Nor need I enter here very fully into the character of the special funeral ceremonies of India. I described them in a special paper, " On Sepulture and Sacrificial ferent jakhas. Thus, according to the GobhiUj.khS, Ymdz. Fiirtyagua. is to be considered as smSxta, not as jrauta (pindz-pitriya^-nzk khalv ssm^-MAakhtiytm nlsti) ; while others maintain that an agnimat should perform the sm-rta, a jr$,ut4gnimat the jrauta Pit^'iya^^a; see Gobhiltya S-rihya-siitras, ^.671. On page 667 we road : anagner ajnivasyJjraddha, n&nvS-haryambj^iidara^ztyajaa.

t^MDA AND VEDANTA. 24^ Customs in the Veda," nearly thirty years ago. * Their spirit is the same as that of the funeral ceremonies of Greeks, Romans, Slavonic, and Teutonic nations, and the coincidences between them all are often most surprising.

In Vedic times the people in India both burnt and buried their dead, and they did this with a certain solemnity, and, after a time, according to fixed rules. Their ideas about the status of the departed, after their body had been burnt and their ashes buried, varied considerably, but in the main they seem to have believed in a life to come, not very different from our life on earth, and in the power of the departed to confer blessings on their descendants. It soon therefore became the interest of the survivors to secure the favor of their departed friends by observances and offerings which, at first were the spontaneous manifestations of human feelings, but which soon became traditional, technical, in fact, ritual.
zh: 11. 「父親們啊，你們已為火神阿格尼（Agni）所吞沒，來此地坐上你們的席位吧，仁慈的引導者們！享用我們置於草皮上的祭品，然後賜予我們財富與強壯的後嗣！」
12. 「噢，阿格尼！噢，六次讚頌者啊！*應我們的請求，你已將祭品運送，並先使其變得芬芳。你將其獻給先祖們，他們享受了他們的份額。噢，神啊，你也當進食這些獻上的祭禮吧。」
13. "在此處的眾父者，以及不在此處的眾父者，那些我們所知道的，與那些我們所不知道的，汝火祭之知者（Gatavedas），知其數之幾何，願受此善制之祭禮與祭祀之份額！"
14. 「對於那些在天堂中分享喜樂的人——無論是否經歷火的焚燒——哦王啊，願汝賜予他們所渴望的生命，使其身體得以承受！」† 這種獻祭對象乃是原始祖先，有別於此者，乃是自古以來兒女對其亡故父親應當表示的敬畏之情，不久後亦及於祖父與曾祖父。這些更具個人性質的感受所表現的儀式具有更家族化的特徵，因此允許更多的地方差異性。

要在此處甚至略述保存於《梵書》（Brahma«as）、《吠陀經軌》（Srauta, G//hya, and SamayaMrika Sutras）、《法書》以及眾多後出的儀式手冊中那些細緻的規定，其實是完全不可能的。這些手冊全都旨在尊敬亡者，內容涵蓋了時間和季節的精確規範、祭壇和祭品的詳細規定、祭祀器具的數量和形狀、祭祀者應有的姿勢，以及器具的各種排列方式。這一切如此繁瑣，以至於我們極難把握真正關切的問題，即那些最初設計這一切複雜儀式的人們的思想和意圖。歐洲學者也已針對這類祭祀著述甚豐，始於柯爾布魯克（Colebrooke）的傑出論文集《印度人的宗教儀式》（"The Religious Ceremonies of the Hindus"），首次發表於《亞洲研究》（Asiatic Researches）第五卷，加爾各答，一七九八年。然而，當我們提出一個簡單的問題──究竟是什麼思想促生了這一切外在的儀式形式，以及它似乎滿足了人類內心何種自然的渴望──我們幾乎在任何地方都得不到明確的答案。誠然，《薩拉達祭祀》（Sraddhas）至今仍在整個印度進行著，但我們知道現代的儀式形式與古老的《經典》（Sastras）中所規定的規則已相去甚遠。從近代旅行者的記述中清楚可見，若不懂梵文、不能閱讀古代的《經軌》（Sutras），就根本無法理解甚至這些古老儀式遺存的真正意義。我們確實被詳細地告知了靈魂據說要進食的糕餅是如何製作的、用多少根草莖來承放它們、每根草莖應當多長、應當朝哪個方向拿持。那些對我們毫無啟蒙的細節被解釋得十分充分，而真正的學者真正關心的寥寥數事卻被輕輕帶過，彷彿它們對我們毫無興趣，必須在堆積如山的廢墟下才能發現。
為了獲得一些啟蒙，我認為我們應當區分——
日常的祖先祭祀——皮特里亞祭（Pitryaja）——乃五大祭祀（Mahayajnas）之一；
2. 月祭祖先的祭祀，即毘（*a-pitr/ya^a*），作為朔望月祭（New and Full-moon sacrifice）的一部分；
3. 一家之主去世時的喪禮儀式；
4. 愛筵（Agapes），或稱為愛與慈善的盛宴，通常稱為「舍剌陀」（Sraddha），在這類盛宴中，食物及其他慈善禮物被贈予值得尊敬的人士，以紀念已故的祖先。「舍剌陀」（Sraddha）這個名稱本來只屬於最後這一類，但它已被轉用於第二、第三類的祭祀，因為舍剌陀在這些祭祀中佔有重要的部分。

每日祖靈祭（Pitr/ya^a）或祖先崇拜是五大祭祀之一，有時亦稱為大祭祀（Great Sacrifices），每位已婚男子應當每日進行。《家庭禮儀經》（Gnhyaslatras）（阿闥婆吠陀第三卷第一篇）中提及此五祭，即提婆祭（Devaya^;1a）獻給提婆，畜牲祭（Bhutaya^la）獻給動物等，祖靈祭（Pitnya^;1a）獻給先祖，梵天祭（Brahmaya^-^au）獻給梵天，即吠陀之研習，以及人祭（Manushyaya^a）獻給人類，即款待賓客等。
阿那‧格麗亞經 III. i, 1；帕拉斯卡拉‧格麗亞經 II. 9, 1；阿帕斯坦巴‧法論，比勒爾譯，第47頁及以下。
241 《摩奴法典》（III. 70）告訴我們同樣的事，亦即已婚男子有五項重大的宗教義務應當履行：
梵天祭祀（Brahma-sacrifice），即吠陀的研習與教授（有時亦稱為Ahuta）。
2. 祖靈祭祀（Pitn-sacrifice），即向亡靈（有時稱為 Prai-ita）獻祭糕餅與水。
3. 提婆祭祀（Deva-sacrifice），即向諸神獻祭（有時稱為 Huta）。
4. 不護祭（Bhuta-sacrifice），即施食於眾生之儀式（有時稱為普護祭 Prahuta）。
5. 人類祭祀（Manushya-sacrifice），即以殷勤款待客人（有時稱為梵火祭 Brahmyahuta）。* 這項日常祖靈祭祀（Pitnya）的舉行似乎極為簡樸。家父必須將其聖線（sacred cord）置於右肩，誦念「向祖靈致敬」（Svadha to the Fathers），並將某些祭品的殘餘朝向南方擲出。!

人類進行此類祭祀的衝動——若得稱之為祭祀的話——意圖甚為明確。早期包含在五項「大祭祀」(pañcha-mahá-yajña)中的乃是人類日常生活的全部本分。這些祭祀與其日常飲食息息相關。當此飲食正在準備之時，在他本人可以享用之前，他必須向眾神奉獻某物——瓦依耶-瓦德瓦祭祀(Vaiš-vadeva)，其中主要的神祇為阿耆尼(Agni)火神、蘇摩(Soma)、毘濕婆提婆(Viśve Devas)、丹萬塔利(Dhanvantari)——一種類似於艾斯庫拉庇烏斯(Aesculapius)的神祇、庫希(Kuhii)與阿努瑪蒂(Anumati)(月相的不同顯現)、帕傑帕蒂(Prajpati)——萬物之主。此等祭祀稱為呼塔(Huta)、阿呼塔(ahuta)、波拉呼塔(prahuta)、普拉賈帕提(prajapti)。

我檢視了提供的英文原文，發現文本出現嚴重的字符損壞或掃描錯誤，導致無法辨識其原意。文中「2 42 ^^J'A T CAN INDIA TEA CII USf creatures, Dydvd-pnthivt, Heaven and Earth, and SvislVaknt, the fire on the hearth.」的大部分內容已不可讀。 根據可辨識的片段推測，原文可能涉及吠陀文獻中的宇宙論概念（如「Heaven and Earth」對應梵文 *dyāvāpṛthivī*），但無法準確還原完整句意。 **建議**：請提供清晰的原文影像或文本檔案，以便進行準確翻譯。
在如此祭祀四方天地的提婆之後，一家之主必須向開闊的空氣中投擲一些祭品，這些祭品乃是供奉動物之用，在某些情況下則為無形的生靈、鬼魂及此類存在而獻。其後他應當紀念已逝的祖靈，即毘陀（Pit/s），並奉獻祭品；然而縱然完成這一切，他仍不得開始自己的膳食，除非他也已將某物施與陌生人（atithis）。
當所有這一切都已完成，並且當這位家長（如我們所說）已經誦念了他的日常禱告，或者背誦了他所學過的吠陀內容之後，也只有在那時，他才與周圍的世界相協調，他已經進行了五大祭祀，並且他得以免除了因粗心和自私的生活而產生的一切罪孽。

這個毘多亞（Pitnya）作為五種日常祭祀之一，在《梵書》（Brahma/^as）、《家庭經》（Gn"hya）及《樣式經》（Samaya/^arika Sutras）中有所記載，當然在法律《本集》（Sa;;2hit^s）中也有記載。拉真德拉·拉爾·米特拉* 告知我們，「正統的婆羅門至今聲稱遵守所有這五種儀式，但實際上只有獻祭給諸神與祖先的儀式嚴格遵守，而誦讀僅由重複《迦耶特里》（Gayatri）來完成，慈善與餵養動物則是偶然且不確定的。」
與這種簡單的日常祖先奉獻迥然不同的是 Pitrya 或 Pinda-pitrya，它構成許多法定祭祀的一部分，首先就是朔望月祭（New and Full-Moon sacrifice）。在這裡，人類的動機再次變得十分清楚易懂。

243 正是對自然規律性進程的沉思、對天體起落之秩序的發現、對世界某種統治力量日益增長的信心，將人的思想從日常勞作提升至更高的境界，並在他心中燃起以讚美、感恩與祭祀來親近這些更高力量的渴望。而正是在月盈月虧之類的時刻，他的思想會最自然地轉向那些生命已然衰落、光輝的面容不復顯於人間者——他的先人或祖先。因此，在新月祭（New-Moon sacrifice）之伊始，據《梵書》（Brāhmaṇas）及《施勒陀經》（Śrauta-sūtras）所述，必須進行祖先祭（Pitṛyajña），一種獻祭給祖先的儀式。在南方火祭火（Dakṣiṇāgni）中需準備一種祭糕或餅食，其供品由水與圓形糕餅（piṇḍas）組成，特別獻祭於父、祖父與曾祖父，而若祭祀者之妻欲求子嗣，則允許她食用其中一塊糕餅。類似地，祖先供奉也在其他各種祭祀中進行，其中新月與滿月祭構成了普遍的類型。

這兩種祖先祭祀可能確實有相同的對象並共享相同的名稱，但其性質是不同的；倘若如往往發生的那樣，將其混淆在一起，我們就會喪失最重要的教訓，詳見 O. Donner 博士著《瓦亞薩涅亞-桑希塔》(Vājasaneyi-saṁhita)，第 XIX 卷第 36-37 頌。關於將祭祀對象限制在三位祖先——父親、祖父和曾祖父——的規定，見《瓦亞薩涅亞-桑希塔》(Vājasaneyi-saṁhita)，第 XIX 卷第 36-37 頌。

244 印度能教給我們什麼 古代祭儀的研究應當教給我們什麼。我無法比藉著指出那兩種 Pitr̥yājñas 之間的差異更決然地加以描述，即前者乃由一家之父，或者可以說由一位俗人所舉行，而後者則由一位正式祭司或由祭祀者特地選定代其行事的一個祭司階級所舉行。如同印度人自己所說的，前者是 gr̥hya，一種家庭祭儀，後者是 śrauta，一種祭司祭儀。* 我們現在轉向第三類祭儀，它們同樣是家庭性和個人性的，但卻因其偶發性質而異於前兩種祭儀。我所指的是葬禮，有別於祖先祭儀。在某個方面，這些葬禮可能代表了一個比日常與月度祖先獻祭更早的崇拜階段。它們引導走向前者，並且可以說預備了亡者，使其將來獲得 Pitr̥s 或祖先的尊位。另一方面，祖先的概念一般而言必定早於任何亡者可被提升至該等級之前已然存在；因此我寧願先描述祖先獻祭。

我無須在此詳細論述印度特殊的喪葬儀式性質。我曾在專門論文《論埋葬與祭祀》中加以描述，內容涉及不同的雅卡（jakhas）。據《高婆利梵經》（Gobhila-Śrihya-sūtras）所言，品達亞加（Pinḍa-pitriyajña）應視為傳統法（smṛta），而非韋陀法（śrauta）（品達品陀祖先祭祀誠然並無韋陀聖火之根據）；而其他學派則主張無火者應行傳統法（smṛta），具韋陀聖火者應行韋陀法（śrauta）品陀祖先祭祀；參見《高婆利梵經》第671節。在第667頁我們讀到：無聖火者祈願，不應奉獻。
古人關於「吠陀中的習俗」的記載，成書於近三十年前。*其精神與希臘人、羅馬人、斯拉夫民族及日耳曼民族的葬禮儀式相同，而這些民族之間的巧合往往令人驚奇。

吠陀時代，印度人既焚燒亡者遺體，亦埋葬其遺骨，且舉行一定之莊嚴儀式，而後來更依循既定之規則進行。他們對於亡者狀態之理解——即其身體已焚、灰燼已埋之後——觀念紛歧，然大體而言，似乎相信來世存在，其狀況與吾人現世生活相去不遠，並且深信已故者具有恩澤其後裔之力量。因此，生者乃有動機籍由種種慣習與祭祀來獲取已故親友之歡心，此等慣習與祭祀起初乃人類感情之自發性表現，然不久即蛻變為傳統、技術性乃至儀軌（ritual）性質。
